executive_summary: IMF Working Paper (Laeven & Valencia, 2026) updates the systemic banking crises database to cover 1970-2025, recording 164 crises (including borderline cases), with 13 new episodes since the 2020 update.

Most new episodes stem from non-financial shocks—particularly the late-2014 oil price collapse hitting oil exporters (Angola, Chad, Azerbaijan, Equatorial Guinea)—rather than financial-sector origins.

The 2023 turmoil (SVB, Signature, First Republic in the US; Credit Suisse in Switzerland) did NOT meet the systemic crisis threshold, as prompt intervention contained contagion and liquidity support stayed below the 5% threshold.

Despite COVID-19, supply shocks and the inflation surge, no wave of systemic crises materialized—credited to post-GFC Basel III buffers (CCyB, capital conservation buffer), LCR, and decisive policy support.

High-income crises tend to last longer with larger output losses (median ~31.75% of GDP) but lower median fiscal costs and NPLs; low/middle-income crises show higher NPLs (median 30%) and shorter durations.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Post-GFC regulatory reforms (Basel III releasable buffers, LCR) materially strengthened banking-system resilience and diversified the crisis-response toolkit, preventing a pandemic-era wave of systemic crises.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Validates that well-capitalized, liquidity-buffered banks can absorb severe macro shocks without state rescue—supportive for bank sector credit profiles in jurisdictions with full Basel III implementation. Opportunity to differentiate issuers by buffer headroom and CCyB releasability. Monitor whether buffers are actually released in downturns versus hoarded, and whether weaker-implementing jurisdictions remain vulnerable; rating implications skew stable-to-positive for compliant banks, negative for laggards.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The 2023 failures (SVB, Signature, First Republic, Credit Suisse) demonstrated that fast deposit runs—driven by uninsured/wholesale deposit concentration and digital withdrawals—can fell individual banks even in advanced systems, yet swift official action prevented systemic escalation.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Interest-rate risk in held-to-maturity portfolios plus concentrated uninsured funding is now a front-line credit risk, especially for mid-sized banks lacking G-SIB scrutiny. Risk: speed of modern runs compresses reaction time for both supervisors and analysts. Rating implication—penalize deposit concentration and unhedged duration risk; reward diversified, insured, sticky funding. Monitor uninsured deposit ratios, AFS/HTM unrealized losses, and deposit beta.

key_takeaways.takeaway: New crisis episodes are increasingly driven by external real-economy shocks (notably the oil price collapse) feeding into bank balance sheets, rather than endogenous financial excess.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: For commodity-dependent and concentrated economies, sovereign and banking risk are tightly linked through the commodity channel—bank asset quality is hostage to terms-of-trade swings. Opportunity to use commodity price scenarios as leading indicators of banking stress. Monitor oil/commodity exposure concentration in loan books, FX-denominated liabilities, and sovereign-bank doom-loop dynamics in frontier markets.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Crisis outcomes diverge sharply by income level: high-income crises last longer with larger output losses but lower NPLs and fiscal costs; low/middle-income crises show much higher NPLs (median 30% vs 11%) but shorter durations.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Implies different recovery and loss-given-default assumptions by jurisdiction class. Larger, deeper financial systems transmit disruption more forcefully to GDP, while EM crises hit asset quality harder but resolve faster. Rating implication—calibrate sovereign and bank recovery timelines and through-the-cycle loss assumptions to income tier. Monitor fiscal space for backstops, as lack of a fiscal backstop prolongs reliance on liquidity over recapitalization.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Extensive central-bank liquidity support is the near-universal first response to crises (median peak liquidity support ~20% of deposits/foreign liabilities), but prolonged reliance can mask solvency problems.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Heavy liquidity provision is a signal of stress, not stabilization—analysts should not read central-bank lifelines as resolution. Risk: low/middle-income countries lean on liquidity far longer before recapitalizing, delaying loss recognition and inflating eventual fiscal costs. Monitor the transition from liquidity support to recapitalization/restructuring as a key inflection in crisis trajectory and fiscal-cost build-up.

entities_impacted.entity: Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, First Republic (US regional banks)
entities_impacted.impact: Failed in 2023 due to concentrated uninsured/wholesale deposits and rapid digital runs; resolved via FDIC (systemic risk exception, full uninsured-depositor protection; First Republic sold to JP Morgan Chase). Did not meet systemic crisis criteria.

G-SIB representing 14.2% of Swiss banking assets failed in March 2023; SNB injected CHF168bn liquidity (4.5% of system liabilities, below 5% threshold); taken over by UBS with a CHF9bn state guarantee. Classified non-systemic due to speedy resolution.
Deployed new liquidity facility, invoked systemic risk exception, and backed uninsured deposits—early intervention prevented spillover; no fiscal restructuring cost incurred.

entities_impacted.entity: Oil-exporting frontier economies (Angola, Chad, Azerbaijan, Equatorial Guinea, Republic of Congo)
entities_impacted.impact: Suffered banking crises triggered by the late-2014 oil price collapse damaging bank balance sheets via deteriorating asset quality; recorded high output losses and NPLs.
2019 systemic crisis with deposit freezes and bank holidays; estimated overall financial-system losses ~US$70bn (over 300% of 2022 GDP), one of the most severe in the sample.
Experienced significant stress but did not fully meet the systemic crisis definition; flagged as borderline for monitoring.

Provide the analytical framework and data; the database underpins cross-country vulnerability assessment, resolution-framework evaluation, and validates the effectiveness of post-GFC Basel III reforms.

monitoring_points: Monitor uninsured/wholesale deposit concentration and HTM/AFS unrealized losses at mid-sized banks because the 2023 episodes showed these drive ultra-fast, fatal deposit runs.
Monitor commodity/oil price trajectories and loan-book concentration in commodity-dependent economies because terms-of-trade shocks now originate most new banking crises.

Monitor the shift from central-bank liquidity support to recapitalization/restructuring because prolonged liquidity reliance often masks underlying solvency problems and inflates eventual fiscal costs.
Monitor Basel III buffer headroom and whether authorities actually release CCyB/capital conservation buffers in downturns because release capacity underpins system resilience.
Monitor borderline cases (Vietnam 2022, Sri Lanka 2023, Nicaragua 2018) and sovereign-bank linkages because they can escalate into full systemic crises.
learning: Liquidity support is a stress signal, not a resolution—median peak liquidity support of ~20% shows central-bank lifelines pervade crises; do not interpret them as stabilization.

Calibrate recovery timelines and loss assumptions by income tier: high-income crises are longer with larger GDP losses but lower NPLs/fiscal costs, while EM crises feature much higher NPLs but faster resolution.
Funding structure is a primary credit driver—concentrated uninsured/wholesale deposits combined with unhedged duration risk can fell a bank within days, irrespective of headline capital ratios.

Real-economy and commodity shocks can be leading indicators of banking distress; integrate terms-of-trade and oil-price scenarios into bank asset-quality stress tests for commodity-exposed jurisdictions.

Strong post-GFC capital and liquidity frameworks (Basel III buffers, LCR) plus available fiscal backstops materially reduce crisis incidence and severity—jurisdictional implementation quality should feed into sovereign and bank ratings.
related_topics: Basel III capital buffers (CCyB, capital conservation buffer)
FDIC systemic risk exception and bank resolution
Central bank liquidity facilities and swap lines
